---
title: "Linear mixed-effects models: structured noise"
---

::: {.callout-tldr title="Fast path"}
When observations come in groups (many scans per subject, many subjects per
site), they are **not independent**, and pretending they are gives wrong error
bars. A **mixed model** adds *random effects* $Zb$ that absorb the group
structure, leaving the per-observation noise white again. The price: the response
now has a **structured covariance** $V_y = \sigma_b^2 ZZ^{\top} + \sigma_e^2 I$
with unknown **variance components** $\theta$ to estimate. The clean way to
estimate them is **REML**, which corrects the same downward bias that makes you
divide a sample variance by $n-1$ instead of $n$. Estimating them naively costs
an $N\times N$ solve per element — hopeless at brain scale — so `nitrix` uses the
**FaST-LMM spectral trick**: eigendecompose the random-effect structure *once*,
rotate into a basis where $V_y$ is **diagonal**, and the per-element fit becomes
elementwise-cheap, with the variances found by a few **average-information Newton**
steps. Those variances are the same object as the
[smoothing penalties](fellner-schall.qmd) of the next chapter.
:::

This is rung 2 of [the ladder](index.qmd). It assumes the [GLM chapter](glm.qmd):
the linear model, least squares, and the small shared solve. It builds directly on
the [definition of variance and covariance](foundations.qmd#sec-variance) from the
foundations chapter, and it leans on the [spectral
solver](solvers.qmd#sec-spectral) and the [Newton machinery](solvers.qmd#sec-newton)
from the shared-machinery page.

## 1. The problem: observations that aren't independent

The GLM chapter assumed each observation carried independent noise. Real designs
break that constantly:

- several runs from the **same subject** are more alike than runs from different
  subjects;
- subjects scanned at the **same site** share scanner quirks.

Ordinary least squares still gives a sensible $\hat\beta$, but its *standard
errors* are wrong — it counts correlated observations as if they were independent,
and is overconfident. We need to model the correlation.

Here is the concrete question to keep in mind. We have measured **cortical
thickness** in one voxel, four times per subject (two visits, two hemispheres,
say), for twenty subjects, and we want the population **age slope**. Eighty
numbers — but they are not eighty independent witnesses. The four measurements
from one subject mostly agree with *each other*; they are really closer to twenty
witnesses. If we ignore that and run plain OLS, the age slope's confidence
interval will be far too narrow, and we will declare effects "significant" that a
second cohort would not reproduce. The mixed model is how we count the witnesses
correctly.

## 2. Fixed effects, random effects, and structured noise

Split the linear predictor into two kinds of term:

$$
y = X\beta + Z b + \varepsilon,
\qquad b \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_b^2 I_q),
\qquad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_e^2 I_N).
$$ {#eq-lme}

- $X\beta$ are the **fixed effects** — the population-level coefficients we want
  to estimate (the age slope, a group difference), exactly as in the GLM.
- $Zb$ are the **random effects** — one offset $b_j$ per group (per subject, per
  site), drawn from a shared distribution. $Z$ is an indicator matrix saying which
  group each observation belongs to. We do not estimate each $b_j$ as a free
  parameter; we estimate the single **variance** $\sigma_b^2$ that says how much
  groups differ.

The distinction is the whole idea: treat a factor with many levels not as many
coefficients but as *draws from one distribution*, summarised by its variance.
Twenty subject offsets become **one number**, $\sigma_b^2$ — "how much do subjects
differ from each other?".

Because $b$ is random, fold it into the noise. Using the rule that
$\operatorname{Cov}(Mb) = M\operatorname{Cov}(b)M^{\top}$ from the [foundations
page](foundations.qmd#sec-variance), and that $b$ and $\varepsilon$ are
independent, the marginal covariance of $y$ (averaging over the unobserved $b$) is
no longer white:

$$
\operatorname{Cov}(y) \;=\; V_y \;=\; \sigma_b^2\, ZZ^{\top} + \sigma_e^2 I_N .
$$ {#eq-V}

So a mixed model is "the GLM with a **structured covariance** $V_y$ instead of
$\sigma_e^2 I$". Everything else follows from that one change. The two
unknowns $\sigma_b^2$ and $\sigma_e^2$ live inside $V_y$, and the entire rest of
this chapter is about (a) what those unknowns *are*, (b) how to estimate them
without bias, and (c) how to do it fast enough for a brain.

## 3. Variance components: the unknowns inside $V_y$ {#sec-varcomp}

The numbers $\theta = (\sigma_b^2, \sigma_e^2)$ that parameterise $V_y$ are called
the **variance components**. In the general mixed model the marginal covariance is
written $V_y = ZGZ^{\top} + R$, where $G$ ($q\times q$) is the covariance of the
random effects and $R$ ($N\times N$) is the residual covariance; the variance
components are precisely *the unknowns inside $G$ and $R$*. For the one-random-intercept
model @eq-lme they are as simple as they get: $G = \sigma_b^2 I_q$ and
$R = \sigma_e^2 I_N$, so $\theta$ is two numbers and nothing more.

### What the structure looks like

Start from the picture before any algebra. Two measurements from the *same*
subject share that subject's offset $b_j$, so they covary by exactly
$\sigma_b^2$. Two measurements from *different* subjects share no random effect,
so they are uncorrelated. And every measurement carries its own independent noise
$\varepsilon_i$, contributing $\sigma_e^2$ to its own variance. Put those three
sentences together and you have already written $V_y$: a **block-diagonal**
matrix, one block per subject, each block equal to $\sigma_b^2$ everywhere with an
extra $\sigma_e^2$ added down the diagonal. The off-diagonal blocks (different
subjects) are zero. @eq-V is exactly that matrix; $ZZ^{\top}$ is the "same group?"
indicator, $1$ inside a block and $0$ between blocks.

@fig-lme-cov draws it for three subjects of unequal size, so you can *see* the
"subjects are correlated with themselves" structure that makes the noise non-white.

```{python}
#| label: fig-lme-cov
#| fig-cap: "The marginal covariance $V_y=\\sigma_b^2 ZZ^\\top+\\sigma_e^2 I$ for three subjects with 4, 3, and 5 observations. Each diagonal block is one subject: every pair within it covaries by $\\sigma_b^2$ (the bright square), with an extra $\\sigma_e^2$ on the diagonal. Different subjects (off-diagonal blocks) are uncorrelated — black. This block structure is what 'observations aren't independent' means."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
group_sizes = [4, 3, 5]
N = sum(group_sizes)
Z = np.zeros((N, len(group_sizes)))      # one indicator column per subject
row = 0
for j, n in enumerate(group_sizes):
    Z[row:row + n, j] = 1.0
    row += n

sigma_b2, sigma_e2 = 1.0, 0.4
V = sigma_b2 * (Z @ Z.T) + sigma_e2 * np.eye(N)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.6, 4.0))
im = ax.imshow(V, cmap="magma")
edges = np.cumsum([0] + group_sizes)
for e in edges:
    ax.axhline(e - 0.5, color="white", lw=0.8)
    ax.axvline(e - 0.5, color="white", lw=0.8)
ax.set(title=r"marginal covariance $V_y=\sigma_b^2 ZZ^\top+\sigma_e^2 I$",
       xlabel="observation", ylabel="observation")
fig.colorbar(im, ax=ax, shrink=0.85, label="covariance")
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

### The first estimator you would invent — and why it breaks

How would you guess $\sigma_b^2$ and $\sigma_e^2$ from data, with no theory? The
natural move is **method-of-moments / ANOVA**, and it is worth doing because it is
the intuition REML will refine. Within a subject, all the spread is noise, so the
pooled within-subject variance estimates $\sigma_e^2$. Across subjects, the spread
of the *subject means* reflects **both** the between-subject variance $\sigma_b^2$
*and* the leftover noise in each mean (a mean of $n_j$ noisy values still has
variance $\sigma_e^2/n_j$). So you estimate the total spread of the means and
*subtract off* the part you already know is noise:

$$
\hat\sigma_b^2 \;=\; \underbrace{\operatorname{Var}(\text{group means})}_{\text{between spread}}
\;-\; \underbrace{\overline{\sigma_e^2/n_j}}_{\text{noise still in a mean}} .
$$ {#eq-mom}

That is a real, usable estimator — the shrinkage figure later in this chapter uses
exactly @eq-mom. But it has two flaws that motivate everything that follows.
**First, it can go negative.** If the subjects happen to look similar, the
subtraction in @eq-mom can produce a negative number, which is nonsense for a
variance; you have to clamp it at zero (the code does `max(..., 0.1)`). **Second,
it ignores the cost of estimating $\beta$.** The "spread of the means" pretends we
know the population mean, but we estimated it from the same data — and as we will
see in @sec-reml, that wastes degrees of freedom and biases the variance *down*.
Method-of-moments is the right intuition with the wrong bookkeeping. REML fixes the
bookkeeping.

### Variance components beyond the two-number case

In `nitrix` the variance-component machinery is deliberately general. The shared
REML core, `lme/_varcomp.py`, never hard-codes "two variances": it works with a
covariance written as a sum of $K$ components, $\operatorname{diag}(V_y) = \sum_k
\sigma_k^2\, B_{k} + \text{offset}$, where each $B_k$ is one component's diagonal
contribution in a basis where $V_y$ is diagonal (see @sec-fastlmm). The
one-random-intercept fit (`reml_fit`) instantiates this diagonal core with $K=2$
and $B = [\lambda,\, 1]$ and `offset` $=0$; FLAME (@sec-flame) with $K=1$, $B =
[\mathbf 1]$ and `offset` $=$ the known within-subject variance. The richer designs
of @sec-beyond — nested, crossed, random slopes — cannot be diagonalised by a single
rotation, so they do not use this diagonal core; instead they share only the *Newton
recipe* (the analytic-curvature `damped_newton`, and for crossed the same
`VarCompSpec` iteration budget), each carrying its own structure-exploiting objective
over its own three (nested/crossed) or block (random-slope) variance components. The
residual itself can be *structured* rather than a
plain $\sigma_e^2 I$: `lme/_corr.py` lets $R = \sigma_e^2 R(\rho)$ carry a
within-group correlation parameter (AR(1) in time, compound symmetry), and
`lme/_varfunc.py` lets the residual *scale* vary across observations,
$\operatorname{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma_e^2 g_i^2$ — heteroscedasticity by a
power of a covariate (`var_power`) or per-stratum (`var_ident`). Both are folded in
by **whitening**: divide each group's data by its own $R^{1/2}$ (or by $g_i$), and
on the whitened data the residual is back to i.i.d., so the same variance-component
fit applies, with one extra $\log$-determinant term in the objective for the
whitening Jacobian. The correlation/scale parameters then join $\theta$ in the
same Newton vector and are estimated alongside the variances.

## 4. REML: estimating variances without the downward bias {#sec-reml}

We now know *what* to estimate ($\theta$) and have a crude estimator (@eq-mom). To
do it properly we maximise a likelihood — with one essential refinement.

### The bias, by analogy you already know

You already accept one bias correction without thinking about it. The sample
variance divides by $n-1$, not $n$:

$$
\hat\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_i (x_i - \bar x)^2 .
$$

Why $n-1$? Because you used the data *twice*: once to estimate the mean $\bar x$,
and again to measure spread *around that same estimated mean*. The points sit
closer to their own sample mean than to the true mean, so $\sum (x_i-\bar x)^2$ is
systematically too small; dividing by $n-1$ instead of $n$ exactly compensates for
the one degree of freedom spent on $\bar x$.

The identical thing happens in a mixed model, but worse: we spend $p$ degrees of
freedom estimating the whole fixed-effect vector $\hat\beta$, and then measure
residual spread around that fitted $X\hat\beta$. Plain maximum likelihood ignores
this and **under-estimates the variances** — the more fixed effects, the worse the
bias. **Restricted maximum likelihood (REML)** is the mixed-model generalisation of
the $n-1$ correction: it estimates $\theta$ from a version of the likelihood that
has the fixed effects *projected out*, so no degrees of freedom are silently
double-counted.

### The objective, term by term

Concretely, REML maximises the likelihood of the residual contrasts — the part of
the data orthogonal to the column space of $X$, which carries no information about
$\beta$ but all the information about $\theta$. Profiling $\beta$ out analytically
(at any fixed $\theta$ the best $\hat\beta$ is the generalised least squares
solution, below) leaves an objective in $\theta$ alone. Writing it as a negative
log-likelihood to minimise:

$$
\text{nll}(\theta) = \tfrac12\Big[\,\underbrace{r^{\top}V_y^{-1}r}_{\text{data fit}}
\;+\; \underbrace{\log|V_y|}_{\text{complexity}}
\;+\; \underbrace{\log|X^{\top}V_y^{-1}X|}_{\text{REML correction}}\,\Big],
\qquad r = y - X\hat\beta(\theta).
$$ {#eq-reml}

Read the three terms as three forces:

- $r^{\top}V_y^{-1}r$ is the **data-fit** term — generalised, $V_y$-weighted
  residual sum of squares. It pushes the variances *up*: inflating $V_y$ makes any
  residual cheap. On its own it would send $\sigma^2\to\infty$.
- $\log|V_y|$ is the **complexity / Occam** term. The determinant of $V_y$ is the
  volume the model is willing to spread probability over; a bigger $V_y$ has a
  bigger $\log|V_y|$. It pushes the variances *down*. The optimum balances these
  two — exactly the variance level that makes the residuals look "typical" under
  $V_y$.
- $\log|X^{\top}V_y^{-1}X|$ is **REML's signature term**, and it is precisely the
  $n-1$ correction in disguise. $X^{\top}V_y^{-1}X$ is the precision (inverse
  covariance) of $\hat\beta$; its log-determinant is the penalty for having spent
  data on estimating $\beta$. Delete this one term and @eq-reml becomes ordinary ML
  — and the variance estimates come out biased low. This term is the whole
  difference between REML and ML.

Once $\hat\theta$ is found, the fixed effects come from **generalised least
squares** — least squares with $V_y^{-1}$ as the weight, the GLM's
[normal equations](glm.qmd#sec-irls) with $W = V_y^{-1}$:

$$
\hat\beta = (X^{\top}V_y^{-1}X)^{-1} X^{\top}V_y^{-1}y .
$$ {#eq-gls}

`nitrix` parameterises $\theta = \log(\sigma^2)$ so the optimisation is
unconstrained and the variances stay positive automatically — so the
negative-variance pathology of @eq-mom cannot occur at all.

@fig-lme-reml is the loss surface itself: @eq-reml evaluated on a grid of
$(\sigma_b^2, \sigma_e^2)$, with the optimum (the bottom of the bowl) marked. It is
this surface that `reml.py` walks downhill.

```{python}
#| label: fig-lme-reml
#| fig-cap: "The REML objective @eq-reml over the two variance components, for one simulated random-intercept voxel. Contours are the restricted negative log-likelihood; the red star is its minimum (the REML estimate), the white dot the true variances used to simulate the data. The surface is smooth and has a single well — which is why a handful of Newton steps suffice. The valley is gently diagonal: the data constrain the *total* variance well but trade off between its between- and within-group split, exactly the ridge that biases naive ML."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
M, n = 8, 6                                   # groups, obs per group
N = M * n
sigma_b2_true, sigma_e2_true = 1.5, 0.6
Z = np.repeat(np.eye(M), n, axis=0)           # (N, M) group indicators
X = np.ones((N, 1))                           # intercept-only fixed effect
b = rng.normal(0, np.sqrt(sigma_b2_true), M)
eps = rng.normal(0, np.sqrt(sigma_e2_true), N)
y = X @ np.array([5.0]) + Z @ b + eps         # beta_true = 5
ZZt = Z @ Z.T

def reml_nll(sb2, se2):
    V = sb2 * ZZt + se2 * np.eye(N)
    Vi = np.linalg.inv(V)
    XtViX = X.T @ Vi @ X
    beta = np.linalg.solve(XtViX, X.T @ Vi @ y)
    r = y - X @ beta
    _, logdetV = np.linalg.slogdet(V)
    _, logdetXtViX = np.linalg.slogdet(XtViX)
    return 0.5 * (logdetV + logdetXtViX + r @ Vi @ r)   # eq-reml

SB, SE = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(0.05, 4.0, 80), np.linspace(0.05, 2.0, 80))
surf = np.vectorize(reml_nll)(SB, SE)
imin = np.unravel_index(np.argmin(surf), surf.shape)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.4, 4.2))
ax.contour(SB, SE, surf, levels=25, cmap="viridis")
ax.plot(SB[imin], SE[imin], "r*", ms=15, label="REML optimum")
ax.plot(sigma_b2_true, sigma_e2_true, "wo", mec="k", label="truth")
ax.set(xlabel=r"$\sigma_b^2$ (between-group)", ylabel=r"$\sigma_e^2$ (residual)",
       title="REML objective (restricted negative log-likelihood)")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9)
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

### How `reml.py` optimises it

The surface in @fig-lme-reml is smooth and (in $\log$-variance coordinates)
single-welled, so a second-order method converges in a few steps. `nitrix` uses
**Newton scoring** — the same Newton idea as the GLM's IRLS, now on the variance
components — but with two deliberate choices that make it brain-scale, both living
in `lme/_varcomp.py`:

- **Analytic average-information curvature, not an autodiff Hessian.** The exact
  REML gradient (the *score*) is available in closed form, and instead of the true
  Hessian `nitrix` uses the **average-information** matrix (Gilmour, Thompson &
  Cullis 1995) — the average of the observed and expected information. It is cheap,
  closed-form, and guaranteed positive-(semi)definite, so the Newton step always
  points downhill; there is no saddle to guard against and no second-order autodiff
  through a Cholesky. The score and AI matrix are both assembled as reductions over
  the $N$ observations with **no $N\times N$ intermediate** ever formed.
- **Damped and budgeted.** As in the GLM, the step is **damped**
  (Levenberg-style): a small ridge on the AI curvature, plus backtracking, keeps
  the objective decreasing even far from the optimum and near the variance-floor
  boundary where a component is collapsing to zero. A fixed ~20-iteration budget
  (typically converging in 5–10) keeps the whole fit `vmap`-clean across the image,
  via the shared `damped_newton` in `stats/_optimise.py`
  ([Newton machinery](solvers.qmd#sec-newton)).

The fit (`REMLResult`) returns, per voxel, the log-variances $\hat\theta$, the
fixed effects $\hat\beta$ via @eq-gls, the fixed-effect covariance
$(X^{\top}V_y^{-1}X)^{-1}$ (`fixed_cov`) for contrast tests, the covariance of
$\hat\theta$ itself (`theta_cov`, the inverse AI matrix) and the gradient tensors
(`grad_m`) needed for **Satterthwaite degrees of freedom** — because the variances
are *estimated*, the right reference distribution for a fixed-effect $t$-test is a
$t$ with fractional, data-dependent degrees of freedom, which `lme_t_contrast` /
`lme_f_contrast` compute from exactly these tensors. (`lme/reml.py`.)

## 5. The scaling wall, and the FaST-LMM trick that breaks it {#sec-fastlmm}

Look again at @eq-reml: it has a $\log|V_y|$ and a $V_y^{-1}$, and $V_y$ is
$N\times N$. Done naively, each evaluation is $O(N^3)$ — and we repeat it, every
Newton step, for *every voxel*. At hundreds of thousands of voxels this is
hopeless.

The escape (Lippert et al. 2011, **FaST-LMM**) is one eigendecomposition. The
mechanics of *why* an eigendecomposition diagonalises a symmetric matrix live on
the [spectral-solver page](solvers.qmd#sec-spectral); here is the payoff in one
breath. Diagonalise the random-effect structure once, $ZZ^{\top} = U\Lambda
U^{\top}$, and **rotate** the data into that orthonormal basis, $\tilde y =
U^{\top}y$, $\tilde X = U^{\top}X$. Because $U$ is orthogonal it does not change any
least-squares quantity — but in the rotated basis the covariance @eq-V is
*diagonal*:

$$
\tilde V_y = \sigma_b^2\Lambda + \sigma_e^2 I = \operatorname{diag}(d),
\qquad d_i = \sigma_b^2\lambda_i + \sigma_e^2 .
$$ {#eq-rotated}

Now every expensive piece is elementwise: $\log|V_y| = \sum_i \log d_i$,
$V_y^{-1}$ is $\operatorname{diag}(1/d)$, and $X^{\top}V_y^{-1}X = \sum_i \tilde
x_i \tilde x_i^{\top}/d_i$. Per-iteration cost collapses from $O(N^3)$ to
$O(Np^2 + N)$. Crucially, the eigendecomposition depends only on $Z$, which is
**shared across all voxels** — so it is computed **once** at the outer call and
reused for the whole image (the same "compute the shared piece once" pattern as the
GLM's $X^{\top}X$). A scalar random effect that was a brain-melting $N\times N$
solve becomes a handful of vector operations per voxel. This is the mass-univariate
payoff: **eigendecompose once, reuse across the image.**

`nitrix` adds a **low-rank** refinement (`lme/_lowrank.py`). When the number of
random-effect columns $q$ is smaller than $N$ — the usual case, far fewer subjects
than observations — it eigendecomposes the $q\times q$ matrix $Z^{\top}Z$ instead
of the $N\times N$ matrix $ZZ^{\top}$, recovering the same nonzero spectrum and the
range eigenvectors $U_r = ZW/s$ without ever forming the $N\times N$ factor. The
$N-q$ "null" directions all carry the same total variance $\sigma_e^2$ (the random
effect is silent there), so they never need to be materialised individually: they
enter the objective only through three small per-voxel Gram aggregates and a
multiplicity count $n_0 = N-q$. A rank-deficient $Z$ (e.g. empty label levels) is
absorbed by that null space. The one eigendecomposition uses the cuSOLVER-robust
`safe_eigh`; `reml_fit` auto-selects low-rank whenever $q < N$, and the per-voxel
inner loop makes no vendor-solver call at all.

## 6. FLAME: when the within-group variance is known {#sec-flame}

fMRI group analysis has a special, common structure that makes one of the two
variances *disappear*. Each subject's time series is first fit by a level-1 GLM,
yielding a per-subject effect $\beta_i$ **and its standard error** $s_i$ — the
measurement error of that subject's own estimate. The group model is then a
**measurement-error mixed model**:

$$
\beta_i = X_{\text{grp}}\gamma + b_i + \varepsilon_i,
\qquad b_i\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_b^2),\quad \varepsilon_i\sim\mathcal{N}(0,s_i^2),
$$ {#eq-flame}

where the within-subject variance $s_i^2$ is **known** (it came from level 1) and
only the between-subject variance $\sigma_b^2$ is unknown. This is FSL's
**FLAME** model (Beckmann, Jenkinson & Smith 2003; Woolrich et al. 2004).

The intuition for why known-$R$ simplifies everything: in the general model we had
to estimate the whole residual covariance $R = \sigma_e^2 I$, fighting the data-fit
term against the complexity term. Here $R = \operatorname{diag}(s_1^2, \ldots,
s_N^2)$ is *handed to us*. The total per-subject variance is then $\sigma_b^2 +
s_i^2$ — already diagonal, no rotation needed — and the only thing left to estimate
is the single scalar $\sigma_b^2$. So FLAME is a **one-parameter REML**:
`flame_two_level` runs the shared diagonalised-REML core (`_varcomp`) with **one**
free component, basis $B = [\mathbf{1}]$, and a fixed per-observation **offset**
equal to the known $s_i^2$ (`lme/flame.py`). Fitting it as a generic
two-parameter REML would be non-identifiable — a free residual *scale* and a free
$\sigma_b^2$ would trade off, with nothing in the data to separate them — which is
exactly why it gets its own one-parameter solver. (`nitrix` also offers a
`robust=True` Huber-style down-weighting of outlier subjects, FSL's FLAME-outlier
idea, which inflates a suspicious subject's $s_i^2$ before refitting.)

## 7. Beyond one random intercept {#sec-beyond}

A single scalar random intercept is the simplest mixed model and the one the
FaST-LMM path serves. Real designs ask for more, and `nitrix`'s `lme_fit`
dispatches each to the cheapest *exact* solver. The unifying idea throughout: the
random structure dictates the block pattern of $V_y$, and each solver exploits that
pattern so it never forms an $N\times N$ matrix.

### Crossed vs nested random effects

Two grouping factors can relate to each other in two fundamentally different ways,
and the difference shows up directly in $Z$ and in the block structure of $V_y$.

- **Nested** (`lme/_nested.py`): subjects **within** sites — each subject belongs
  to exactly one site. The factors telescope: site, then subject-within-site. The
  random part is $Z_1 b_1 + Z_2 b_2$ with $b_1$ the site offsets and $b_2$ the
  (within-site) subject offsets, so $G$ is block-diagonal with two variances,
  $\theta = (\sigma_1^2, \sigma_2^2, \sigma_e^2)$. Because each subject lives under
  one site, $V_y$ is **block-diagonal across sites**, and *within* a site block it
  is a compound-symmetry-within-subject structure plus a rank-one site term. Both
  levels invert in closed form by **Sherman–Morrison** (the "telescoping
  Woodbury"), assembled from per-sublevel counts and group-sums via `segment_sum`
  — no $N\times N$ factor. (`lme_fit(..., inner=...)` → `NestedLMEResult`.)
- **Crossed** (`lme/_crossed.py`): subjects **×** stimuli — every subject sees
  every stimulus, so an observation has *both* a subject level and a stimulus level
  and the two factors do not nest. Now $V_y = \sigma_1^2 Z_1 Z_1^{\top} +
  \sigma_2^2 Z_2 Z_2^{\top} + \sigma_e^2 I$ is **not** block-diagonal across either
  factor (a subject's rows are tangled with a stimulus's rows), so neither the
  FaST-LMM rotation nor the per-group Woodbury applies. The tractable structure is
  a Woodbury on the stacked design $[Z_1\,|\,Z_2]$ whose inner matrix has two
  diagonal blocks (the per-level counts) joined by the $q_1\times q_2$
  cross-tabulation; a **Schur complement** eliminates the larger factor cheaply and
  leaves a single dense $\min(q_1,q_2)^3$ solve per Newton step. Cheap when one
  factor is small (30 stimuli × 1000 subjects), expensive when both are large.
  (`lme_fit(..., cross=...)` → `CrossedLMEResult`.)

### Random slopes and correlated effects

A scalar intercept says "subjects differ in level". Often they also differ in
**slope** — each subject has their own age trajectory. Then $Z_i = [\mathbf{1},
\text{age}]$ per group, and the per-group random effect is a small *vector*
$b_i\sim\mathcal{N}(0, G)$ with a $2\times 2$ covariance $G$ — its diagonal the
intercept and slope variances, its off-diagonal the **intercept–slope
correlation** ("do subjects who start high rise faster?"). This is `(1 + x | g)`.

This breaks FaST-LMM (one rotation can no longer diagonalise a non-scalar $G$), but
for a *single* grouping factor $V_y$ is still block-diagonal across groups, so a
**per-group Woodbury** keeps the heavy algebra at the tiny $r\times r$ (here $2\times
2$) and $p\times p$ scale (`lme/_blockwoodbury.py`, tier R2). $G$ is carried in
**log-Cholesky** coordinates ($G = LL^{\top}$, log diagonal) so it stays
positive-definite under an unconstrained Newton step. Passing
`structure='diagonal'` constrains $G$ to be diagonal — the uncorrelated `(x || g)`
effect, independent intercept and slope. For designs where the within-group
random covariates are themselves rich, `lme/_lowrank.py` provides the same
low-rank parameterisation used in @sec-fastlmm to keep the per-group cost down.
(`lme/_corr.py` is the companion piece for a *correlated residual* within a group —
AR(1) in time, compound symmetry — folded in by whitening, as in @sec-varcomp.)

### Kenward–Roger small-sample correction {#sec-kr}

There is a subtle inferential bug waiting in every mixed model with **few groups**.
When we test a fixed-effect contrast $c^{\top}\beta = 0$, the standard error comes
from $(X^{\top}V_y^{-1}X)^{-1}$ — but we plugged in $\hat\theta$ as if it were the
*true* $V_y$. It is not; we *estimated* it, and with only, say, twelve subjects we
estimated it poorly. Ignoring that extra uncertainty makes the naive $t$- and
$F$-tests **anti-conservative**: standard errors come out too small, $t$-statistics
too big, $p$-values too optimistic, and you over-reject. (The same disease the $n-1$
correction and REML treat for *estimation*, now reappearing for *inference*.)

**Kenward & Roger (1997)** fix both halves of the problem (`lme/_kr.py`):

1. **An inflated covariance** $\Phi_A$. They add a correction term to
   $\Phi = (X^{\top}V_y^{-1}X)^{-1}$ that accounts for the variability of
   $\hat\theta$ — built from the derivatives of $V_y$ with respect to the variance
   components and the REML information matrix $W$ of $\theta$. The estimate of
   $\beta$ is more uncertain than $\Phi$ alone admits, and $\Phi_A \succeq \Phi$
   restores the missing variance.
2. **Adjusted denominator degrees of freedom.** They moment-match the resulting
   Wald statistic to a scaled $F$-distribution with a reduced, data-dependent
   denominator df $m$ — wider tails, so the critical value is appropriately larger
   for small samples.

For the R1 model $V_y$ is **linear** in the natural components $(\sigma_b^2,
\sigma_e^2)$, which kills the second-derivative term and lets every KR quantity
reduce to diagonal weighted sums in the FaST-LMM rotated basis — closed-form and
cuSOLVER-free. In `nitrix` you reach it with `lme_t_contrast(result, c,
dof='kr', X=X, Z=Z)` (it must re-derive the rotated basis, so the original design
is re-passed); the default `dof='satterthwaite'` is the cheaper large-sample
correction. Use KR when groups are few.

## 8. A worked example: borrowing strength

The signature behaviour of a mixed model is **partial pooling**: a group's
estimate is pulled toward the global mean, the more so when the group is small or
noisy. This is the practical face of the variance components — the shrinkage weight
is built *entirely* from $\sigma_b^2$ and $\sigma_e^2$. Pure NumPy, runs at build
time, using the method-of-moments estimate @eq-mom (the idea REML refines).

```{python}
#| label: fig-shrinkage
#| fig-cap: "Partial pooling. Noisy per-group means (no pooling) are shrunk toward the global mean by the mixed model; small/extreme groups move most. The shrunk estimates sit closer to the truth — the printed RMSE confirms it."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
M = 20                                   # groups
true_mu = 5.0
b = rng.normal(0, 1.0, M)                # true group offsets, sigma_b = 1
group_true = true_mu + b
n = rng.integers(2, 12, M)               # unequal group sizes
sigma_e = 2.0
ybar = np.array([rng.normal(group_true[j], sigma_e/np.sqrt(n[j])) for j in range(M)])

# variance components (method-of-moments, the idea REML refines)
grand = ybar.mean()
s_b2 = max(ybar.var(ddof=1) - (sigma_e**2 / n).mean(), 0.1)
s_e2 = sigma_e**2
shrink = (n * s_b2) / (n * s_b2 + s_e2)          # per-group weight on its own mean
partial = grand + shrink * (ybar - grand)         # empirical-Bayes estimate

j = np.argsort(ybar)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 3.4))
ax.axhline(grand, color="0.6", lw=1, ls=":", label="global mean")
ax.plot(range(M), ybar[j], "o", label="no pooling (group mean)")
ax.plot(range(M), partial[j], "s", label="partial pooling (mixed)")
ax.plot(range(M), group_true[j], "x", color="k", label="truth")
for i, jj in enumerate(j):
    ax.plot([i, i], [ybar[jj], partial[jj]], color="0.8", lw=1, zorder=0)
ax.set(xlabel="group (sorted)", ylabel="estimate", title="partial pooling shrinks toward the mean")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, ncol=2)
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
rmse = lambda e: np.sqrt(np.mean((e - group_true)**2))
print(f"RMSE  no-pool={rmse(ybar):.3f}   partial-pool={rmse(partial):.3f}")
```

The shrinkage weight $\frac{n_j\sigma_b^2}{n_j\sigma_b^2 + \sigma_e^2}$ is exactly
the ratio of variances $V_y$ encodes. A large group ($n_j$ big) or a large
between-group variance ($\sigma_b^2$ big) trusts its own mean; a small or
low-variance group is pulled hard toward the centre. Estimating
$\sigma_b^2,\sigma_e^2$ well — what REML does — is what makes the pooling right.

## Where this connects

Rewrite the shrinkage weight's denominator and a familiar quantity appears: the
ratio $\sigma_e^2/\sigma_b^2$ is a **penalty strength**. Shrinking a group's
offset toward zero is the same operation as penalising a coefficient toward zero
with a penalty matrix $S$. That is not a coincidence — it is the
**penalty–variance equivalence** the next chapter makes precise: a random effect
*is* a penalised coefficient whose penalty strength is a variance ratio, and the
penalty matrix $S$ of a [GAM](gam.qmd#sec-penalty) plays the role of $G^{-1}$ here.
Which is why the GAM machinery and this LME machinery are two faces of one method,
and why the [Fellner–Schall](fellner-schall.qmd#sec-fs-update) update can estimate
both with the same step.

## Further reading

- Patterson, H. D. & Thompson, R. (1971). *Recovery of inter-block information when
  block sizes are unequal*. **Biometrika** 58(3), 545–554. (The original REML.)
- Harville, D. A. (1977). *Maximum likelihood approaches to variance component
  estimation and to related problems*. **JASA** 72(358), 320–338. (Variance-component
  computation; the restricted likelihood as a likelihood of error contrasts.)
- Gilmour, A. R., Thompson, R. & Cullis, B. R. (1995). *Average information REML*.
  **Biometrics** 51, 1440–1450. (The closed-form curvature `nitrix` uses.)
- Lippert, C., Listgarten, J., Liu, Y., Kadie, C. M., Davidson, R. I. & Heckerman,
  D. (2011). *FaST linear mixed models for genome-wide association studies*.
  **Nature Methods** 8, 833–835. (The spectral rotation of @sec-fastlmm.)
- Kenward, M. G. & Roger, J. H. (1997). *Small sample inference for fixed effects
  from restricted maximum likelihood*. **Biometrics** 53, 983–997. (The df
  adjustment of @sec-kr.)
- Pinheiro, J. C. & Bates, D. M. (2000). *Mixed-Effects Models in S and S-PLUS*.
  Springer. (Random slopes, nested/crossed designs, correlation and
  variance-function structures.)
- Bates, D., Mächler, M., Bolker, B. & Walker, S. (2015). *Fitting linear
  mixed-effects models using lme4*. **Journal of Statistical Software** 67(1),
  1–48.
- Woolrich, M. W., Behrens, T. E. J., Beckmann, C. F., Jenkinson, M. & Smith, S. M.
  (2004). *Multilevel linear modelling for fMRI group analysis using Bayesian
  inference*. **NeuroImage** 21(4), 1732–1747. (FLAME, the known-within-variance
  group model of @sec-flame.)
- Beckmann, C. F., Jenkinson, M. & Smith, S. M. (2003). *General multilevel linear
  modeling for group analysis in fMRI*. **NeuroImage** 20(2), 1052–1063.

## See also

- Code:
  [`nitrix/stats/lme/reml.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/lme/reml.py)
  (`reml_fit`, `lme_fit`, the contrast tests),
  [`lme/_varcomp.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/lme/_varcomp.py)
  (the shared AI-REML core),
  [`lme/flame.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/lme/flame.py),
  [`lme/_lowrank.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/lme/_lowrank.py),
  [`lme/_nested.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/lme/_nested.py),
  [`lme/_crossed.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/lme/_crossed.py),
  [`lme/_blockwoodbury.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/lme/_blockwoodbury.py),
  [`lme/_corr.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/lme/_corr.py),
  [`lme/_varfunc.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/lme/_varfunc.py),
  [`lme/_kr.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/lme/_kr.py),
  and the shared `stats/_optimise.py` damped Newton.
- Foundations: [variance and covariance](foundations.qmd#sec-variance);
  [spectral solver](solvers.qmd#sec-spectral) and
  [Newton machinery](solvers.qmd#sec-newton).
- Previous rung: [generalised linear models](glm.qmd). Next rung:
  [generalised additive models](gam.qmd) — the same penalty, applied to
  *smoothness* instead of group membership.
